death.notes: Per memorial: 'He departed this life in joyful hope of a blessed immortality through Jesus Christ November 5, A.D. 1842, Aged 72 Years.'

burial.plot_notes: Reinterred December 10, 1870 from original Wilkes-Barre burial. Joint memorial with wife TD0238.

parents.father_name: Col. Joel Jones of Hebron CT
parents.father_research_notes: Col. Joel Jones of Hebron CT — son's namesake (Joel Jr) was Judge Joel Jones TD0239 Philadelphia mayor. Not built.

narrative_blocks.body: Born Hebron CT October 17, 1770, son of Col. Joel Jones of Hebron. Married Elizabeth Hale Huntington of Coventry CT November 9, 1794 and resided at Coventry until 1816, when the family removed to Wilkes-Barre PA — part of the post-1800 New England migration into the Wyoming and Susquehanna Valleys.

death.notes: Per memorial: 'He departed this life in joyful hope of a blessed immortality through Jesus Christ November 5, A.D. 1842, Aged 72 Years.'

burial.plot_notes: Reinterred December 10, 1870 from original Wilkes-Barre burial. Joint memorial with wife TD0238.

parents.father_name: Col. Joel Jones of Hebron CT
parents.father_research_notes: Col. Joel Jones of Hebron CT — son's namesake (Joel Jr) was Judge Joel Jones TD0239 Philadelphia mayor. Not built.

narrative_blocks.body: Born Hebron CT October 17, 1770, son of Col. Joel Jones of Hebron. Married Elizabeth Hale Huntington of Coventry CT November 9, 1794 and resided at Coventry until 1816, when the family removed to Wilkes-Barre PA — part of the post-1800 New England migration into the Wyoming and Susquehanna Valleys.

death.notes: Per Huntington Family book: 'his widow [died] April 16, 1843' — five months after Amasa. Per joint memorial inscription: 'aged 69 years' (so birth corrected per Coventry CT Aug 22, 1774).

burial.plot_notes: REINTERRED December 10, 1870 from original Wilkes-Barre PA burial. Joint memorial with husband Amasa Jones. Reinterment likely facilitated by son Matthew Hale Jones (1811-1883), lawyer at Easton — deferred.

marriages.notes: Per Huntington Family in America 1915: m. 'Nov. 9, 1794, Amasa Jones of Coventry... son of Col. Joel Jones, of Hebron, Conn.' 9 children; 6 living as of 1858 per the book. Only TD0239 Judge Joel Jones built this stage. 8 children deferred (Talcott gen 7 cohort): Rev Joseph Huntington Jones DD 1797-1868 (Sixth Presbyterian Philadelphia, Harvard 1817), Fanny Huntington 1799-1893 (Easton PA), Margaret Emeline 1801-1806 child death, Maria Allis 1803-1884 (m. William Allis Westfield NY), Eliza Wright 1805-1854 St Louis (m. Joseph J. Wright US Army surgeon), Dr Samuel H. Jones 1808-1864, Mary Joanna Harris 1809-1837 (m. Rev O. Harris), Matthew Hale Jones 1811-1883 Easton PA lawyer (→ daughter Elizabeth Huntington Jones Kirkpatrick m. US Congressman William Sebring Kirkpatrick).

narrative_blocks.header: Family moved Coventry Connecticut to Wilkes-Barre 1816
narrative_blocks.body: Born Coventry CT August 22, 1774 to Rev Joseph Huntington and his Talcott-descendant second wife Elizabeth Hale Huntington. Married Amasa Jones of Coventry — son of Col Joel Jones of Hebron CT — November 9, 1794 and bore nine children where her father preached. In 1816 the family relocated to Wilkes-Barre PA in the Wyoming Valley.

narrative_blocks.header: Reinterred Easton Pennsylvania twenty-seven years later
narrative_blocks.body: Died Wilkes-Barre PA April 16, 1843 age 68, five months after her husband Amasa. Both were originally buried Wilkes-Barre. December 10, 1870 — twenty-seven years after her death — both bodies were transferred to Easton Cemetery PA, where son Matthew Hale Jones lived as a lawyer.

narrative_blocks.header: Half-sister of Ohio Governor Samuel H. Huntington
narrative_blocks.body: Through father Rev Joseph Huntington's first marriage to Hannah Devotion, she had a half-brother Samuel H. Huntington (1765-1817) who would become the third Governor of Ohio 1808-1810 and chief justice of the Ohio Supreme Court. He was nine years her senior; she was the first of Joseph's nine M2 children with her Talcott-descendant mother.

bio.title: Judge
bio.extra_names: Named for paternal grandfather Col. Joel Jones of Hebron CT.

birth.notes: Wikipedia + biographical dictionary give Oct 26, 1795; FindAGrave gives Oct 25, 1795 — minor discrepancy. Using Wikipedia/canonical Oct 26.

marriages.notes: Per Wikipedia: 'June 14, 1831 married Eliza P. Sparhawk in Philadelphia, with whom he had six children.' Per FindAGrave 5 children named: Rev John Sparhawk Jones (TD0240 1841-1910 — built next stage); Elizabeth Jones (d.1837 young); Joel Jones (d.1837 young); Mary Jones (d.1844 young); Sarah Jones (d.1849 young). The Wikipedia '6 children' may include an unnamed sixth or a Rev John discrepancy. Only Rev John Sparhawk Jones survived to adulthood per all accounts.

education.notes: Graduated Yale College 1817 with high honor at age 22 — INST022 in tree.

education.school_name: Apprenticeship with Judge William Bristol of New Haven CT
education.notes: Studied law under Judge William Bristol of New Haven CT after Yale.
Litchfield Law School 1819 under Tapping Reeve — completed legal training before being admitted to the bar in New Haven.

career.notes: Conducted an academy at Wilkes-Barre PA for a year and a half starting 1822 — after family crisis prompted his relocation from New Haven CT.
Practiced law at Easton PA for many years before relocating to Philadelphia.

Secretary to Lafayette College board of trustees 1826-1835; continued as trustee through 1852. (Lafayette College not yet built as INST — pending Kirkpatrick line stage which will add 4+ connections including grandnephew William Sebring Kirkpatrick.)

1830 appointed by Governor George Wolf as one of three commissioners — alongside William Rawle and Thomas I. Wharton — to revise the civil code of Pennsylvania. Published 1834 'A Syllabus of the Law of Land Office Titles in Pennsylvania.' Report submitted to PA Senate January 15, 1836.

Associate Judge Philadelphia District Court 1835-1845; Presiding Judge 1845-1847. Resigned 1847 to accept Girard College presidency. Notable Delaware River fisheries ruling upholding 1783 PA-NJ compact + private fishery rights as protected property despite federal navigation/commerce supremacy.

First president of Girard College 1847-1849. Girard's 1831 will founded the college for 'poor white male orphans' with controversial clause barring clergymen from campus (upheld by SCOTUS in Vidal v. Girard's Executors 1844).

Mayor of Philadelphia October 16, 1849 to October 15, 1850. Elected as Independent (some sources Democratic) candidate October 9, 1849 narrowly defeating Whig Charles Gilpin. Lost 1850 reelection to same Gilpin. Single-year term under pre-1854 city charter.
Elected member American Philosophical Society 1848. (APS not yet built as INST.)

Ruling elder Presbyterian Church. Theological writings: Notes on Scripture (1860 posthumous, published by widow); Story of Joseph or Patriarchal Age (Girard College student use); Knowledge of One Another in the Future State; edited The Literalist (5 vols 8vo prophetic literature with original additions); translated 'Outlines of a History of the Court of Rome and of the Temporal Power of the Popes' from French. Advocate of literal Second Coming prophecy.

institutions.institution_blurb: Yale College B.A. 1817 with high honor.

notable.notable_blurb: Mayor of Philadelphia 1849, Girard College first president

cross_connections.display_label: Federal Judge Eastern District PA; great-grandnephew via brother Matthew

narrative_blocks.header: Mayor of Philadelphia 1849, narrow Independent victory
narrative_blocks.body: Elected Mayor of Philadelphia October 9, 1849 as the Independent (or Democratic) candidate, narrowly defeating Whig attorney Charles Gilpin to break the Whig Party's longstanding hold on the executive office. Inaugurated October 16, 1849 under the pre-1854 single-year charter. Lost his 1850 reelection bid to the same Gilpin amid the city's nativist Whig-Democrat ferment that would later fuel the Know-Nothing movement.

Resigned the presiding judgeship of Philadelphia District Court 1847 to become the first president of Girard College — Stephen Girard's 1831 endowed institution for 'poor white male orphans.' Served two years amid lingering controversy from the Supreme Court's 1844 Vidal v. Girard's Executors decision upholding Girard's will clause barring clergymen from campus. Stepped down 1849 to enter the mayoral race.

narrative_blocks.header: Commissioned to revise Pennsylvania's civil code, 1830
narrative_blocks.body: Appointed by Governor George Wolf in 1830 as one of three commissioners — alongside William Rawle and Thomas I. Wharton — to revise the Commonwealth's civil code. The team's report to the Pennsylvania Senate January 15, 1836 produced enduring statutory compilations.

Graduated Yale College 1817 with high honor at age 22 — the eldest of nine Coventry CT children. Apprenticed in law under Judge William Bristol of New Haven 1817-1819 before completing at Tapping Reeve's Litchfield Law School 1819. Admitted to the New Haven bar before family crisis prompted his relocation to Pennsylvania, where he conducted a Wilkes-Barre academy 1822-1823 and then practiced law at Easton PA for over a decade.

narrative_blocks.header: Scholar of prophecy and literal Second Coming
narrative_blocks.body: Ruling elder of the Presbyterian Church, with theological writings including the posthumous 'Notes on Scripture' (1860) and 'Story of Joseph or Patriarchal Age' for Girard College students. Edited five volumes of English prophetic literature as 'The Literalist' with extensive original additions, advocating literal interpretation of scriptures predicting the Second Coming of Christ — and translated 'Outlines of a History of the Court of Rome' from the French.

naming_inspiration.description: Named for paternal grandfather Col. Joel Jones of Hebron CT (Amasa's father). Joel Sr not built as separate entry — only known through Amasa's parentage.

parents.mother_research_notes: Elizabeth Smith Sparhawk (?-1838), second wife of Dr John Sparhawk X02863. Marriage record not located. Not built. M1 was Aletheia Webster (1779).

bio.title: Reverend
bio.bio_blurb: Founding minister of Brown Memorial, Baltimore

burial.plot_notes: Immanuel Episcopal Church Cemetery, Sparks MD — family burial ground for Sparhawk-Jones / Turnbull / Kidder descendants.

cross_connections.display_label: Pastor Sixth Presbyterian Philadelphia 1838-1861; theological author

narrative_blocks.header: Founding minister of Baltimore's Brown Memorial Presbyterian
narrative_blocks.body: Pastored Brown Memorial Presbyterian Church Baltimore — the Bolton Hill congregation built around the memorial endowment for George Brown, the Baltimore-Ohio Railroad co-founder. Taught history and was an early advocate for women's higher education before being called to Calvary Church Philadelphia.

narrative_blocks.header: The only child of five to survive
narrative_blocks.body: Mayor Joel Jones of Philadelphia lost four children young in the 1830s and ‘40s; this son alone survived to adulthood, and became a minister. He founded Baltimore’s Brown Memorial Presbyterian, endowed for a B&O Railroad founder.
Beyond the pulpit he taught history and argued for women’s right to it — a Presbyterian minister advocating their higher education when few did. His own daughter Elizabeth became a noted painter.

burial.plot_notes: Easton Cemetery, Easton PA — family burial ground for the Jones-Kirkpatrick Easton branch.

career.notes: Practiced law at Easton PA for over four decades, residing in Easton from young adulthood until his death.

narrative_blocks.header: Likely arranged 1870 reinterment of his parents
narrative_blocks.body: Almost certainly facilitated the December 10, 1870 reinterment of his parents Elizabeth and Amasa Jones from Wilkes-Barre to Easton Cemetery — twenty-seven years after his mother's death. The reinterment placed the founding generation alongside his own household in the Northampton County town where he practiced law for over forty years.

narrative_blocks.header: Anchor of the Easton Pennsylvania Jones branch
narrative_blocks.body: Followed the family migration as a child from Coventry Connecticut to Wilkes-Barre Pennsylvania in 1816 and settled in Easton as a young lawyer. His three descendants — Robert Innes, Elizabeth, and Matthew Hale Sr — produced the Kirkpatrick branch including a US Congressman, a Federal Judge, and a Paris Prize architect.

burial.plot_notes: Easton Cemetery, Easton PA — alongside husband Matthew.

parents.father_research_notes: Innes surname passed forward via firstborn son Robert Innes Jones (1843-1898).

bio.extra_names: Named for paternal grandmother Elizabeth Hale Huntington Jones

burial.plot_notes: Easton Cemetery, Easton PA — alongside husband William Sebring.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name 'Huntington' carries the Talcott-line Huntington surname forward through her paternal grandmother Elizabeth Hale Huntington Jones.

narrative_blocks.header: Bore two notable sons one year apart
narrative_blocks.body: Bore William Huntington Kirkpatrick in October 1885 and Donald Morris Kirkpatrick in March 1887 — just seventeen months apart. The first would become a Federal Judge appointed by Calvin Coolidge, the second a Paris Prize Beaux-Arts architect; both Lafayette College graduates following their father onto its board.

burial.plot_notes: The Woodlands Cemetery, Philadelphia — plot I-14.

education.notes: Princeton Theological Seminary — Presbyterian ministerial training.

career.notes: Pastor of Sixth Presbyterian Church Philadelphia 1838-1861 — twenty-three-year pastorate. Sixth Presbyterian was a major Old School Presbyterian congregation in Philadelphia during the antebellum era.

Author of six published theological works between 1839 and 1864: Outline of the Work of Grace (1839); Influence of Physical Causes on Religious Experience (1846); Life of Ashbel Green VDM (1849); Man Moral and Physical (1860); Autobiography of William Neill (editor, 1861); The Brazen Serpent (1864).
Secretary of the Presbyterian Relief Fund for Disabled Ministers 1861-1868, after stepping down from Sixth Presbyterian pulpit.

institutions.institution_blurb: Honorary Doctor of Divinity (DD), Lafayette College, 1843 — conferred while his brother Joel Jones (TD0239) was a Lafayette trustee.

cross_connections.display_label: Brown Memorial Baltimore founding pastor; later Calvary Philadelphia

narrative_blocks.header: Pastor Sixth Presbyterian Philadelphia, twenty-three years
narrative_blocks.body: Pastor of Sixth Presbyterian Church Philadelphia from 1838 to 1861 — a twenty-three-year tenure at a major Old School Presbyterian congregation. Stepped down 1861 to become Secretary of the Presbyterian Relief Fund for Disabled Ministers, serving in that role until his 1868 death.

His 1849 'Life of Ashbel Green VDM' became the definitive nineteenth-century treatment of Ashbel Green (1762-1848), eighth president of Princeton 1812-1822 and one of the major figures of early-American Presbyterianism. Joseph's biographical scholarship extended also to editing the 1861 Autobiography of Rev. William Neill, Dickinson College president 1824-1829.

narrative_blocks.header: Honorary Doctor of Divinity, Lafayette College, 1843
narrative_blocks.body: Received an honorary Doctor of Divinity from Lafayette College 1843 — conferred while his older brother Joel Jones was still a Lafayette trustee. Two Jones brothers thus held Lafayette honors a generation apart: Joel as founding-era board secretary and trustee, Joseph as honorary DD recipient.

burial.plot_notes: Likely The Woodlands Cemetery Philadelphia plot I-14 alongside husband — to be verified.

parents.father_research_notes: Howell family of Woodbury NJ (Gloucester County). Prominent southern New Jersey family. Specific parents not yet identified.

burial.plot_notes: The Woodlands Cemetery, Philadelphia — same cemetery as father Rev. Joseph Huntington Jones DD (TD0255, plot I-14).
